pathways.summary: Utahs Clean Slate process applies to certain acquittals, dismissals, plea-in-abeyance dismissals, lower-level misdemeanors, infractions, and drug-possession cases. Case Type Automatic Timing / Rule
Acquittal on all charges Goal: 60 days after acquittal for qualifying post-May 1, 2020 cases
Dismissal with prejudice Goal: 180 days after dismissal/final appeal, unless dismissed after plea in abeyance

Certain traffic cases Automatic deletion, not always normal expungement
The Utah Courts public guidance says the automatic process can take up to 120 days, because the court system runs automatic expungement checks periodically and must allow prosecutor response time. (
"https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
Some Utah cases may clear automatically, but not every dismissed or misdemeanor case qualifies. If the case has not cleared automatically, a petition may still be available.
Automatic Expungement: Acquittal or Dismissal With Prejudice

A Utah case may be automatically expunged if it resulted in an acquittal on all charges or was dismissed with prejudice. The statute gives the court a goal of expunging qualifying acquittals 60 days after acquittal and qualifying dismissals with prejudice 180 days after dismissal or final nonappealable order. A dismissal with prejudice after successful completion of a plea in abeyance is excluded from that favorable-outcome automatic category.
Was the case dismissed with prejudice, dismissed without prejudice, dismissed after a plea in abeyance, or did you win an acquittal?
Clean Slate Eligible Convictions and Plea-in-Abeyance Dismissals

Clean Slate automatic expungement can apply to cases where each conviction or charge is one of the lower-level categories allowed by statute: controlled-substance possession misdemeanor, Class B misdemeanor, Class C misdemeanor, or infraction. The automatic waiting periods are generally 5 years for Class C misdemeanors/infractions, 6 years for Class B misdemeanors, and 7 years for Class A controlled-substance possession misdemeanors.
Clean Slate Case Type Waiting Period
Class C misdemeanor or infraction 5 years
Class A misdemeanor controlled-substance possession 7 years

Automatic Clean Slate is narrower than petition-based expungement. Utah excludes many categories from automatic relief, including felonies, most Class A misdemeanors other than drug possession, assault, DUI, domestic-violence-related offenses, sex offenses, and certain weapons offenses. (
A case may fail Utah Clean Slate automatic screening but still be worth reviewing for petition-based expungement.
Petition-Based Expungement With BCI Certificate of Eligibility

Most Utah adult expungement petitions require a Certificate of Eligibility from the Utah Bureau of Criminal Identification, known as BCI. BCI reviews the persons total criminal history, including other states and previous expungements, not just what appears in the Utah BCI file. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungements/")
The petition process generally works like this:
1 Apply to BCI for a Certificate of Eligibility.
2 Submit fingerprints, valid identification, and the BCI application fee.
3 If BCI approves, receive a certificate or special certificate.
4 File the petition in the correct Utah court within 180 days of certificate issuance.

A dismissal with prejudice after successful completion of a plea in abeyance is excluded from that favorable-outcome automatic category.

The automatic waiting periods are generally 5 years for Class C misdemeanors/infractions, 6 years for Class B misdemeanors, and 7 years for Class A controlled-substance possession misdemeanors.
Class C misdemeanor or infraction 5 years
Class A misdemeanor controlled-substance possession 7 years
4 File the petition in the correct Utah court within 180 days of certificate issuance.

BCIs current FAQ says the initial application fee is $65, fingerprints are required even if BCI already has fingerprints, BCI can take up to 1-2 months to respond, and eligible conviction, plea-in-abeyance, or special certificates may require an additional $65 per case; no certificate issuance fee is required for dismissals, acquittals, or declinations. Court filing fees are separate, and fee waivers may be available through the court. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungements/expungements-faq/")

If the person was arrested or charged but not convicted, Utah may allow petition-based expungement of records of arrest, investigation, and detention. A BCI certificate can be issued if at least 30 days have passed after arrest or charge and one of the statutory outcomes applies: no charges will be filed, all charges were dismissed with prejudice, charges dismissed without prejudice or condition with prosecutor consent or 180 days passed, acquittal on all charges, or statute of limitations expired.
Misdemeanor DUI under 41-6a-501(2) 10 years
Certain serious controlled-substance driving injury/death felony 10 years
Felony drug-possession offense 5 years
Class C misdemeanor or infraction 3 years

Utah separates traffic cases from regular criminal expungement. Some traffic offenses are automatically deleted by the court, including traffic acquittals, dismissals with prejudice, and certain adjudicated Class C/infraction or Class B traffic cases after statutory time periods. Automatic deletion uses 5 years for Class C misdemeanor/infraction traffic cases and 6 years for Class B misdemeanor traffic cases.

A person can also petition for traffic expungement without a BCI certificate. Petition-based traffic expungement can use shorter timing: 3 years after adjudication or dismissal for Class C misdemeanor/infraction traffic cases and 4 years for Class B misdemeanor traffic cases, if there is no pending traffic case, no pending traffic plea in abeyance, and the person is not currently on probation for a traffic case.

A dismissal with prejudice after successful completion of a plea in abeyance is excluded from that favorable-outcome automatic category.

A person is not eligible for this non-conviction certificate if they have a pending misdemeanor/felony criminal proceeding, pending misdemeanor/felony plea in abeyance, are currently incarcerated/on parole/on probation, or have a criminal protective order or criminal stalking injunction in effect for the case, subject to limited traffic/minor exceptions.

If BCI denies eligibility, a person may need to explore the Utah Board of Pardons and Parole. If BCI denies a certificate, a pardon or Board of Pardons route may be the next review path, but that is separate from ordinary court expungement.

Utah has a separate vacatur-related expungement process. sourceSections.title: Core Utah Rule
sourceSections.text:
When a Utah court expunges a case, the record is sealed from public access, and the person may generally answer questions as if the arrest, investigation, detention, prosecution, or conviction did not occur, unless another law or court order requires a different answer. Utah Courts explains that expungement applies to government records, not news stories or private copies. ("https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
Wilma should use this plain-language explanation:

Utah expungement can remove the case from ordinary public background checks, but it does not erase news articles, private background-check copies, or every government-access exception.

sourceSections.title: Main Utah Expungement Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Path A - Automatic Clean Slate Expungement
Utahs Clean Slate process applies to certain acquittals, dismissals, plea-in-abeyance dismissals, lower-level misdemeanors, infractions, and drug-possession cases. As of January 1, 2026, the court no longer uses the temporary request form that applied from October 1, 2024 through December 31, 2025; now the court identifies and clears eligible automatic-expungement cases on its own. ("https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
Automatic expungement can apply to:

Case Type Automatic Timing / Rule
Acquittal on all charges Goal: 60 days after acquittal for qualifying post-May 1, 2020 cases
Dismissal with prejudice Goal: 180 days after dismissal/final appeal, unless dismissed after plea in abeyance
Clean Slate eligible conviction or dismissal After statutory waiting period and court identification
Certain traffic cases Automatic deletion, not always normal expungement
The Utah Courts public guidance says the automatic process can take up to 120 days, because the court system runs automatic expungement checks periodically and must allow prosecutor response time. (

"https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
Wilma should say:

Some Utah cases may clear automatically, but not every dismissed or misdemeanor case qualifies. If the case has not cleared automatically, a petition may still be available.

Path B - Automatic Expungement: Acquittal or Dismissal With Prejudice
A Utah case may be automatically expunged if it resulted in an acquittal on all charges or was dismissed with prejudice. The statute gives the court a goal of expunging qualifying acquittals 60 days after acquittal and qualifying dismissals with prejudice 180 days after dismissal or final nonappealable order. A dismissal with prejudice after successful completion of a plea in abeyance is excluded from that favorable-outcome automatic category.
Wilma should ask:

Was the case dismissed with prejudice, dismissed without prejudice, dismissed after a plea in abeyance, or did you win an acquittal?

Path C - Clean Slate Eligible Convictions and Plea-in-Abeyance Dismissals
Clean Slate automatic expungement can apply to cases where each conviction or charge is one of the lower-level categories allowed by statute: controlled-substance possession misdemeanor, Class B misdemeanor, Class C misdemeanor, or infraction. The automatic waiting periods are generally 5 years for Class C misdemeanors/infractions, 6 years for Class B misdemeanors, and 7 years for Class A controlled-substance possession misdemeanors.

Clean Slate Case Type Waiting Period
Class C misdemeanor or infraction 5 years
Class B misdemeanor 6 years
Class A misdemeanor controlled-substance possession 7 years
Automatic Clean Slate is narrower than petition-based expungement. Utah excludes many categories from automatic relief, including felonies, most Class A misdemeanors other than drug possession, assault, DUI, domestic-violence-related offenses, sex offenses, and certain weapons offenses. (

"https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
Wilma should say:

A case may fail Utah Clean Slate automatic screening but still be worth reviewing for petition-based expungement.

Path D - Petition-Based Expungement With BCI Certificate of Eligibility
Most Utah adult expungement petitions require a Certificate of Eligibility from the Utah Bureau of Criminal Identification, known as BCI. BCI reviews the persons total criminal history, including other states and previous expungements, not just what appears in the Utah BCI file. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungements/")
The petition process generally works like this:
1 Apply to BCI for a Certificate of Eligibility.
2 Submit fingerprints, valid identification, and the BCI application fee.
3 If BCI approves, receive a certificate or special certificate.
4 File the petition in the correct Utah court within 180 days of certificate issuance.
5 Court notifies the prosecutor.
6 Prosecutor and victim may object.
7 Court grants or denies the petition. ("https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
BCIs current FAQ says the initial application fee is $65, fingerprints are required even if BCI already has fingerprints, BCI can take up to 1-2 months to respond, and eligible conviction, plea-in-abeyance, or special certificates may require an additional $65 per case; no certificate issuance fee is required for dismissals, acquittals, or declinations. Court filing fees are separate, and fee waivers may be available through the court. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungements/expungements-faq/")
Wilma should say:

A BCI certificate does not finish the expungement. It only allows you to file the petition with the court.

BCI confirms this too: after receiving the certificate, the person must take the certificate to the court of judgment and get an expungement order from the judge. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungements/expungements-faq/")

Path E - Petition-Based Non-Conviction Expungement
If the person was arrested or charged but not convicted, Utah may allow petition-based expungement of records of arrest, investigation, and detention. A BCI certificate can be issued if at least 30 days have passed after arrest or charge and one of the statutory outcomes applies: no charges will be filed, all charges were dismissed with prejudice, charges dismissed without prejudice or condition with prosecutor consent or 180 days passed, acquittal on all charges, or statute of limitations expired.
A person is not eligible for this non-conviction certificate if they have a pending misdemeanor/felony criminal proceeding, pending misdemeanor/felony plea in abeyance, are currently incarcerated/on parole/on probation, or have a criminal protective order or criminal stalking injunction in effect for the case, subject to limited traffic/minor exceptions.
Wilma should ask:

Were charges ever filed, and if so, were they dismissed with prejudice, dismissed without prejudice, declined, acquitted, or still pending?

Path F - Petition-Based Conviction Expungement
For convictions, Utah requires payment of all court-ordered fines, interest, and restitution for the conviction being expunged. The waiting period is measured from the latest of conviction, release from incarceration, release from parole, or release from probation.

Conviction Type Petition Waiting Period
Misdemeanor DUI under 41-6a-501(2) 10 years
Certain serious controlled-substance driving injury/death felony 10 years
Felony, generally 7 years
Felony drug-possession offense 5 years
Class A misdemeanor 5 years
Class B misdemeanor 4 years
Class C misdemeanor or infraction 3 years
Wilma should calculate from:

the latest date among conviction, release from jail/prison, release from parole, or release from probation.

Path G - Convictions Barred From Certificate Eligibility
Utah bars certificate eligibility for several serious categories, including:
Barred Category Wilma Rule
Capital felony Not eligible through ordinary certificate
First-degree felony Not eligible through ordinary certificate
Violent felony Not eligible through ordinary certificate
Felony DUI under 41-6a-501(2) Not eligible through ordinary certificate
Sex, kidnap, or child-abuse registry offense Not eligible if registration was required at sentencing or application
Pending misdemeanor/felony case Blocks eligibility, except certain traffic matters
Pending plea in abeyance Blocks eligibility, except certain traffic matters
Currently incarcerated, on parole, or on probation Blocks eligibility, except certain infraction/traffic/minor regulatory exceptions
Protective order / stalking injunction in effect Blocks eligibility
False or misleading BCI application Blocks eligibility and may be a criminal offense
These bars appear in Utah Code 77-40a-303.
Wilma should escalate all barred or near-barred cases to attorney review.

Path H - Numerical Criminal-History Limits
Utah does not evaluate only the one case the user wants expunged. BCI looks at the persons full criminal history, including previously expunged convictions. Utah has numerical limits that can block eligibility, including:
Criminal History Pattern General Effect
2 or more non-drug-possession felonies from separate criminal episodes Usually blocks eligibility
3 or more non-drug-possession convictions including 2 Class A misdemeanors from separate episodes Usually blocks eligibility
4 or more non-drug-possession convictions including 3 Class B misdemeanors from separate episodes Usually blocks eligibility
5 or more non-drug-possession convictions of any degree from separate episodes Usually blocks eligibility
3 or more felony drug-possession convictions from separate episodes Usually blocks eligibility
5 or more drug-possession convictions from separate episodes Usually blocks eligibility
Utah has special 10-year relief rules that can relax some numerical limits, and BCI may disregard infractions, traffic offenses, minor regulatory offenses, and clean-slate-eligible cases already automatically expunged for certain eligibility calculations.
Wilma should say:

Utah eligibility depends on your whole criminal history, not just the case you want cleared.

Path I - Traffic Offense Expungement / Deletion
Utah separates traffic cases from regular criminal expungement. Some traffic offenses are automatically deleted by the court, including traffic acquittals, dismissals with prejudice, and certain adjudicated Class C/infraction or Class B traffic cases after statutory time periods. Automatic deletion uses 5 years for Class C misdemeanor/infraction traffic cases and 6 years for Class B misdemeanor traffic cases.
A person can also petition for traffic expungement without a BCI certificate. Petition-based traffic expungement can use shorter timing: 3 years after adjudication or dismissal for Class C misdemeanor/infraction traffic cases and 4 years for Class B misdemeanor traffic cases, if there is no pending traffic case, no pending traffic plea in abeyance, and the person is not currently on probation for a traffic case.
Wilma should not treat DUI as a normal traffic case. Utah Courts and BCI both warn that DUI is not considered a traffic offense for these purposes. (

"https://bci.utah.gov/clean-slate-expungement/")

Path J - Cannabis Possession Petition Without BCI Certificate
Utah allows a petition for expungement of a conviction related to cannabis possession without a BCI certificate if the petition shows that, at the time of the arrest or citation, the person had a qualifying medical condition and the cannabis possession was in a form and amount to medicinally treat that condition.
Wilma should ask:

At the time of the cannabis possession case, did you have a medical condition that would qualify for medicinal cannabis, and was the cannabis in a form and amount tied to treatment of that condition?

This is a legal-detail-heavy pathway and should be escalated if the facts are unclear.

Path K - Pardon-Based Expungement
If BCI denies eligibility, a person may need to explore the Utah Board of Pardons and Parole. BCIs forms page includes a Board of Pardons expungement order application and notes that people denied through BCI may contact the Board of Pardons and Parole for further information on pardoned expungements. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungement-forms-and-applications/")
Wilma should say:

If BCI denies a certificate, a pardon or Board of Pardons route may be the next review path, but that is separate from ordinary court expungement.

Path L - Vacatur / Human Trafficking-Related Expungement
Utah has a separate vacatur-related expungement process. BCIs forms page lists an Application for Vacatur Expungement, and Utah Code 77-40a-402 describes how a person who receives a vacatur order must deliver it to affected agencies and attach the BCI application/fingerprints for processing. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungement-forms-and-applications/")
Wilma should treat this as a mandatory attorney or survivor-legal-services escalation pathway.

Path M - Juvenile Expungement
Utah juvenile expungement is separate from adult expungement. BCIs forms page says juvenile courts require a certificate of the persons adult Utah criminal history report before applying for juvenile record expungement, and using the adult expungement application for a juvenile matter can delay the process. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungement-forms-and-applications/")
Wilma should ask:

Was this handled in juvenile court or adult criminal court?

If juvenile:

Use the juvenile expungement process, not the adult BCI certificate process.

Vacatur / trafficking-related.
Pardon-based.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state.
Step 2 - Apply to BCI, unless the pathway does not require it
Most adult non-traffic/non-cannabis petition cases require a BCI Certificate of Eligibility. BCI requires an application, fingerprints, valid ID, and fee, and BCI may process by mail or email. ("https://bci.utah.gov/expungements/expungements-faq/")
Step 3 - File in the correct court
The petition is filed in the court where the criminal case was filed. If that court no longer exists, the petition is filed in the court that would have jurisdiction now. If charges were never filed, the petition is filed in the district court in the county where the arrest occurred or citation was issued.
Step 4 - Watch the 180-day certificate deadline
A BCI certificate or special certificate is valid for 180 days. The court may not accept a petition if the certificate is no longer valid.
Step 5 - Prosecutor and victim notice
The court gives notice to the prosecuting office. For conviction cases and plea-in-abeyance dismissals, the prosecutor must make reasonable efforts to notify any victim. Prosecutors have 35 days to object or recommend, victims have 60 days to object or recommend, and the petitioner may respond within 14 days after receiving an objection or AP&P response.
Step 6 - Hearing or no hearing
If an objection is filed, the court schedules a hearing. If no objection is received within 60 days after filing, the court may grant the expungement without a hearing.
Step 7 - Court standard
The court issues an expungement order if it finds by clear and convincing evidence that the certificate is valid, statutory requirements are met, case-specific requirements are met, and the public interest would not be harmed.
Step 8 - Certified copies and agency processing
The petitioner may request certified copies of the order within 28 days after the court issues it. After the court issues an order, BCI notifies affected agencies and forwards a copy to the FBI; agencies must expunge records once they receive the order, but an order does not restrict an agencys ordinary use or dissemination until that agency receives the order.

sourceSections.title: Effects and Limits of Utah Expungement
sourceSections.text:
After expungement, the person may generally answer questions as if the arrest, investigation, detention, prosecution, or conviction did not occur, unless another law or court order says otherwise. Agencies must generally restrict or deny access to expunged records, but agencies also keep, index, and maintain expunged records, and expunged records may be released to specific entities or under court order.
Entities that may receive expunged-record information under specific request or legal authority include the Board of Pardons and Parole, POST, federal authorities if required by federal law, the State Board of Education, judicial-office investigative bodies, and certain research entities under confidentiality protections. Prosecutors may also communicate about certain expunged domestic violence, stalking, sex/kidnap/child-abuse, and weapons records in limited ways.
Wilma should say:

Utah expungement is strong, but it has exceptions. Some agencies can still access expunged records for specific legal reasons.

Immigration warning
Utah Courts specifically warns noncitizens to get advice from an immigration lawyer before expunging criminal records because the FBI may still have records even after expungement. ("https://www.utcourts.gov/en/self-help/case-categories/criminal-justice/expunge.html")
Wilma should always escalate immigration cases.

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand possible Utah expungement pathways. This is legal information, not legal advice. Utah has automatic Clean Slate expungement, petition-based expungement through BCI, traffic-specific deletion, cannabis possession relief, juvenile expungement, and special vacatur or pardon pathways.

Core Questions
1 Was this case in Utah?
2 Was it handled in adult court, juvenile court, federal court, tribal court, military court, or another state?
3 Which court handled the case?
4 What is the case number?
5 Was the outcome:
Arrest only?
No charges filed?
Acquittal?
Dismissal with prejudice?
Dismissal without prejudice?
Plea in abeyance?
Conviction?
Pardon?
Vacatur?
6 What was the charge and statute?
7 Was it a felony, Class A misdemeanor, Class B misdemeanor, Class C misdemeanor, infraction, traffic offense, DUI, drug possession, or cannabis possession?
8 Was it domestic violence, assault, sex-related, weapons-related, stalking, child-abuse-related, or registry-related?
9 Was there a victim?
10 Is there a criminal protective order, civil protective order, or stalking injunction still in effect?
11 Are any misdemeanor or felony cases pending?
12 Is any plea in abeyance pending?
13 Is the person currently incarcerated, on parole, or on probation?
14 When were conviction, incarceration, parole, and probation fully completed?
15 Are all fines, interest, and restitution paid?
16 How many total convictions does the person have, including other states and prior expungements?
17 Has the case already been automatically expunged?
18 Is the person a noncitizen or dealing with immigration?
19 Is the expungement needed for employment, housing, school, licensing, education, law enforcement, firearms, immigration, or professional certification?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If juvenile
Route:

Use Utah juvenile expungement. Do not use the adult expungement application. The juvenile court requires an adult Utah criminal history report from BCI.

If federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state
Route:

Utah state expungement cannot directly clear this. The user needs the jurisdiction where the case occurred.

If acquitted on all charges
Route:

Check automatic expungement. For qualifying post-May 1, 2020 cases, the statutory goal is 60 days after acquittal.

If dismissed with prejudice
Route:

Check automatic expungement. For qualifying post-May 1, 2020 cases, the statutory goal is 180 days after dismissal or final appeal. If the dismissal was after plea in abeyance, do not use the simple dismissed-with-prejudice automatic path.

If dismissed without prejudice
Route:

Check BCI certificate or Clean Slate pathway. If petition-based, prosecutor consent or 180 days after dismissal may be required.

If plea in abeyance
Route:

Check whether the plea was successfully completed and dismissed. Some plea-in-abeyance dismissals may qualify for Clean Slate timing, but others require petition analysis.

If conviction
Route:

Check fines, interest, restitution, sentence completion, offense level, waiting period, disqualifying offense categories, pending cases, protective orders, and criminal-history numerical limits.

If traffic offense
Route:

Check traffic deletion or traffic petition rules. No BCI certificate is needed for a traffic expungement petition. Do not treat DUI as ordinary traffic.

If DUI
Route:

Misdemeanor DUI has a 10-year waiting period. Felony DUI is barred from ordinary certificate eligibility. Escalate.

If cannabis possession
Route:

Check cannabis possession petition without BCI certificate if the person had a qualifying medical condition and the cannabis was in a form and amount to medicinally treat the condition.

If serious felony / registry / violent felony
Route:

Likely barred from ordinary certificate eligibility. Attorney review or pardon pathway required.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Attorney Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend legal help if any of these apply:
Noncitizen or immigration issue.
Felony conviction.
First-degree felony or capital felony.
Violent felony.
Felony DUI.
Misdemeanor DUI.
Sex, kidnap, or child-abuse registry issue.
Domestic violence.
Stalking.
Weapons offense.
Assault or other offense against the person.
Protective order or stalking injunction still active.
Pending misdemeanor or felony charge.
Pending plea in abeyance.
Currently incarcerated, on parole, or on probation.
Restitution unpaid.
Multiple convictions near or above numerical limits.
Cannabis medical-condition claim.
Human trafficking / vacatur issue.
Pardon needed.
Juvenile/adult court confusion.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state case.
User wants guaranteed outcome.

9. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

Your record will be destroyed.

Nobody can ever see it again.

Clean Slate clears every misdemeanor.

DUI is just a traffic offense.

A BCI certificate means you are done.

The judge must grant it because BCI approved it.

This fixes immigration.

This restores gun rights.

This removes news stories.

This removes private background-check copies automatically.

You can ignore unpaid restitution.

A dismissed case always clears automatically.

A plea in abeyance dismissal is the same as any other dismissal with prejudice.

Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a Utah expungement pathway. The next step is to verify the official court record, BCI record, final disposition, sentence completion, unpaid fines or restitution, pending-case status, and whether the case is automatic, petition-based, traffic-specific, cannabis-specific, juvenile, or a special vacatur/pardon matter.

sourceSections.title: Utah Quick Reference
sourceSections.text:
Topic Utah Rule
Main law Utah Code Title 77, Chapter 40a
Meaning of expunge Seal, restrict, or deny access; not destruction
Automatic Clean Slate Court identifies eligible cases as of Jan. 1, 2026
Automatic acquittal goal 60 days for qualifying post-May 1, 2020 cases
Automatic dismissal with prejudice goal 180 days, excluding plea-in-abeyance dismissal
Clean Slate Class C / infraction 5 years
Clean Slate Class B 6 years
Clean Slate Class A drug possession 7 years
Petition Class C / infraction conviction 3 years
Petition Class B conviction 4 years
Petition Class A conviction 5 years
Petition felony drug possession 5 years
Petition felony generally 7 years
Petition misdemeanor DUI 10 years
Felony DUI Barred from ordinary certificate
First-degree felony / capital felony Barred from ordinary certificate
Violent felony Barred from ordinary certificate
Registry offense Barred from ordinary certificate
BCI certificate validity 180 days
BCI application fee $65 currently listed by BCI
Prosecutor objection window 35 days
Victim objection window 60 days
Petitioner reply window 14 days
No-objection grant May be granted after 60 days without hearing
Certified copies Request within 28 days after order
Traffic petition No BCI certificate; 3 or 4 years depending level
Traffic automatic deletion 5 or 6 years depending level
DUI Not ordinary traffic
Cannabis possession Possible petition without BCI certificate if medical-condition rule met
Immigration Always attorney review
Private records/news Not cleared automatically
